# Vaccines

A vaccine trains the immune system to recognize a pathogen without causing the
disease. The first vaccine was developed by Edward Jenner in 1796, who used
cowpox to protect against smallpox. Vaccines work by exposing the body to an
antigen, prompting it to produce antibodies and memory cells. Widespread
vaccination led to the global eradication of smallpox, declared in 1980. Herd
immunity occurs when enough of a population is immune to slow disease spread.
